title: A Solid-State Ozone Sensor Based on Solid Polymer Electrolyte
abstract:
A Solid-State Ozone Sensor Based on Solid Polymer Electrolyte
Lei Xie, Juntao Lu,* and Heqing Yan
Department of Chemistry, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, P.R. China
Received: March 19, 1998
Final version: June 19, 1998
Abstract
A solid-state amperometric sensor based on solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) for monitoring ozone in gaseous media is reported. The compact sensor
is convenient for practical applications. The sensitivity of the sensor is around 1 mA /ppm cm2 with a 95 % response time of a few seconds,
depending on the structural details of the sensor. The lower detection limit is estimated to be 0.2 ppm (S/N ¼ 3).
Keywords: Ozone, Solid polymer electrolyte, Gas sensor
1. Introduction
Ozone is a strong oxidizing agent and has been extensively
utilized in chemical industries, food storage and clinical steriliza-
tion. Because of its high instability, ozone must be generated on site
at the treatment facility and this requires an adequate sensor system
capable of in situ and instantaneous measurements. Although a
number of techniques have been established for the determination
of ozone [1–3], new advanced methods with high sensitivity, fast
response, easy operation and at low cost are still desirable.
There have been a number of reports on electrochemical ozone
sensors [2–8]. Most electrochemical sensors are Clark type which
have a gas permeable membrane to hold a thin layer of electrolyte
solution in front of the working electrode. Schiavon and co-workers
reported an ozone sensor based on Naﬁon 417 [2]. They used a
porous Au electrode deposited on the Naﬁon membrane as the
working electrode. Unlike the Clark sensors, there was no gas
permeable membrane and the sensitivity was therefore improved
with a smaller temperature coefﬁcient. However, they used liquid
HClO4 solution as electrolyte and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode.
These features make it inconvenient to integrate the sensor into a
portable instrument.
This paper reports on a solid-state ozone sensor based on our
experience in developing solid-state oxygen and carbon monoxide
sensors using Naﬁon as solid electrolyte (SPE) [9, 10]. Our goal was
to make an ozone sensor without liquid solution. Because the
volume of Naﬁon membrane is very small (about 0.2 mm thick), for
long term service, the reactions must cause neither consumption of
the sensor components nor accumulation of reaction products in the
sensor. In the sensor, ozone is to be reduced at the working
electrode and in acidic media it occurs as follows [11]:
O3 þ 2e þ 2Hþ →O2 þ H2O
If the counter electrode is made from platinum or gold, the reaction
occurring at the counter electrode will be
2H2O →O2 þ 4Hþ þ 4e
Therefore, the total reaction in the sensor is
2O3 →3O2
The net reaction is ozone being changed into oxygen through the
sensor, leaving no changes to the sensor itself. This indicates that
ozone detection based on the above mentioned reaction meets the
requirement for a solid-state SPE sensor.
In order to separate the current waves of oxygen and ozone
reductions, Au has been chosen for the working electrode material.
Because the ozone reduction reaction is rather irreversible, the
working electrode should have a large real surface area and proper
structure. Besides, a suitable electrolyte should be selected.
Schiavon and co-workers used porous Au electrode as the working
electrode and found HClO4 to be the best electrolyte for ozone
reduction. This implies that the liquid solution could penetrate
Naﬁon to some extent and the real electrochemical interfaces were
those between Au and HClO4. Our approach to the solid-state SPE
sensor is to absorb a small amount of acid in the SPE to form
effective electrochemical interfaces. In this case, perchloric acid is
not suitable because of its volatility. According to our experience in
developing SPE oxygen sensor and carbon monoxide sensor,
sulfuric acid is a proper choice for retaining active electrochemical
interfaces. A carefully controlled amount of sulfuric acid absorbed
in the Naﬁon-electrodes’ assembly has been proved to allow those
sensors to work normally under relative humidity (RH) ranging
30 %–95 % with a service life over 2 years [9]. However, sulfuric
acid is less favorable than perchloric acid for ozone reduction, most
probably owing to the stronger adsorption of sulfate anions than that
of perchloric anions. The way to remedy this is by improving the
structure of the porous Au working electrode.
There have been a number of different ways for preparing the
porous gold layer. Some basic factors should be taken into
account. First of all, the electrode should have sufﬁcient surface
area where ozone reduction can take place with a sufﬁciently large
apparent rate constant. In this respect, a large total surface area is
favored. However, we have found that residual current and noise
may also increase with increasing total electrochemical interfaces.
Secondly, we noted that not all the electrochemical interfaces are
contributing to ozone reduction. For example, a part of the Au
particles are buried in Naﬁon in the metal-SPE electrode prepared
by chemical reduction using a two-compartment reactor [12]. The
interfaces between Naﬁon and these Au particles are electro-
chemical interfaces but have little contribution to ozone reduction.
Moreover, these interfaces are a source of residual current and
electrochemical noise. Besides, the charge-discharge processes of
the double layer will slow down sensor response. Thirdly,
therefore, for a fast response, the thickness of the porous working
electrode should be small [13]. Some of these requirements are
conﬂicting to each other and certain compromises have to be made
in practice. The method reported in this article seems to be able to
meet the basic requirements so that a prototype of solid-state
ozone sensor can be made. The authors are not claiming this
method to be the best, even for the special case deﬁned by the
topic of the article. The purpose of this article is to demonstrate the
feasibility of a solid-state ozone gas sensor using a metal-Naﬁon
porous electrode. Improvement in performance is certainly possible
in the future.
842
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 12
q WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
1040-0397/98/1209-0842 $ 17.50þ.50/0
 2. Experimental
The conﬁguration of the SPE ozone sensor is shown in Figure 1.
A piece of Naﬁon 117 membrane serves as the solid polymer
electrolyte. The membrane was cleaned by boiling in 3 % H2O2
solution for 0.5 h, in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 solution for 0.5 h and then in
double distilled water for 0.5 h. The working electrode was made
from colloidal Au obtained by reacting 20 mmol/L HAuCl4 with a
mixture of 10 % H2O2 and 60 % PTFE emulsion (at volume ratios
100:20:1). The colloidal Au solution was then dropped on a clean
Teﬂon sheet and dried under an infrared lamp. The composite sheet
was immersed in acetone overnight to remove the surfactant
contained in PTFE emulsion. Finally, the Au layer was transferred
to Naﬁon 117 membrane by pressing the composite sheet to the
membrane and then removing the Teﬂon sheet. To improve the
mechanical strength, the Au layer was wetted with 1–2 mL of 2.5 %
Naﬁon solution (Aldrich) and the membrane wetted with ethanol
before assembling. Then the Au layer and the membrane were
pressed together before drying out and the pressure was kept for
about 10 min. When the above described procedures were followed,
the mechanical stability of the Au electrode-Naﬁon assembly was
found sufﬁcient for sensor applications.
The counter and reference electrodes were both Tefon-bonded Pt
black gas diffusion electrodes, prepared in the way detailed in a
previous article [9] and pressed on the other side of the membrane.
The working and counter electrodes were positioned face to face
across the membrane and had an apparent area about 0.8 cm2. The
reference electrode was put beside the counter electrode. When
facing air, the open circuit potential of the reference electrode is
sufﬁciently stable for sensor operation. The potentials reported
below are all referred to the open circuit air electrode. The
electrodes-SPE assembly was soaked in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 over-
night. After being withdrawn from the acid, the surface of the
assembly was cleaned with ﬁlter paper to remove free solution and
ﬁxed in the Teﬂon case as shown in Figure 1a. Pt wires (f 60 mm)
were pressed on the rims of the electrodes with an inner Teﬂon
cylinder by screw mechanism. The wires were led through the ﬁne
holes in the wall of the case to external connectors. An apparent
area of ca 0.5 cm2 of the working electrode was exposed to the gas
sample.
The ozone source was electrochemically generated mixture of air
and ozone from an SPE electrolizer developed in our laboratory.
The ozone was ﬁrst diluted in air ﬂux to about 1 %. The
concentration was determined with iodometric method. In the
sensor tests, a measured volume of the calibrated gas was injected
into a glass bottle with a syringe for further dilution and used
immediately. In the experiment of ﬂow rate effect, the stored gas
was exported to the sensor by injecting saturated NaCl aqueous
solution into the bottle. In view of the instability of ozone, this
sample gas was freshly prepared and the measurement was ﬁnished
in about ten minutes. The arrangement for gas feeding is shown in
Figure 1b.
3. Results and Discussion
3.1. Steady-State Polarization Curve
Figure 2 shows the steady state current output of the sensor at
ﬂow rate 400 mL/s as a function of the working electrode potential.
With increasing polarization (negative shift of potential), the
cathodic current increases quickly ﬁrst and then reaches a plateau
which is terminated by the onset of oxygen reduction around
¹0.3 V. To allow for possible ﬂuctuation of the reference potential,
a wide potential range for the plateau current is desirable in
practice. The current at the plateau is a limiting current determined
by the mass transport. In general, the measured current I can be
related to the kinetic current Ik and the mass transport limiting
current Im:
1=I ¼ 1=Ik þ 1=Im
ð1Þ
In Equation 1, Ik is a function of the potential and increases with
polarization and the term 1/Ik diminishes at sufﬁciently negative
potentials, resulting in a mass transport limiting current I ¼ Im. The
higher the catalytic activity of the working electrode and the lower
the mass transport limiting current, the wider the plateau. An
acceptable plateau is seen in Figure 2, however, the potential range
needs to be expanded in the future by further optimizing the
working electrode structure. Meanwhile, the working electrode was
kept at ¹0.2 V for ozone sensing.
843
Solid-State Ozone Sensor
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 12
Fig. 1. Schematic drawing of the sensor conﬁguration (a) and the arrangement of gas feeding (b).
Fig. 2. Steady-state polarization curve of the sensor at ozone concentration
27 ppm and gas ﬂow rate 400 mL/min.

Fig. 1. Schematic drawing of the sensor conﬁguration (a) and the arrangement of gas feeding (b).
Fig. 2. Steady-state polarization curve of the sensor at ozone concentration
27 ppm and gas ﬂow rate 400 mL/min.
3.2. The Effects of Flow Rates
It was found in experiments that the sensor response was
inﬂuenced by the ﬂow rate to an extent depending on the sensor
conﬁguration. When the working electrode was directly facing the
ﬂowing sample gas, the response was fast with a 90 % response time
below 1 s and the sensitivity was high. However, in this case the
current output was strongly ﬂow rate dependent and, therefore,
inconvenient for practical applications. One way to reduce the ﬂow
rate dependence is to introduce a diffusion barrier in front of the
working electrode. With such a barrier, the mass transport will be
controlled by two processes linked in series, i.e., the diffusion
through the barrier and the convectional diffusion in the bulk gas
phase. Thus, the mass transport limiting current may be related to
the limiting current of the diffusion through the barrier (Id) and the
limiting current of the convectional diffusion in the ﬂowing gas (Ic)
It is seen from Equation 2 that Im is inﬂuenced by the ﬂow rate in
general. However, when 1=Id q 1=Ic, Im will be practically
independent of the ﬂow rate. The more resistive the diffusion
barrier, the less ﬂow rate sensitive the sensor response. Figure 3
shows the ﬂow rate dependence of sensor response for two different
gas diffusion barriers which were perforated Teﬂon sheets 50 mm
(curve A) and 0.5 mm (curve B) thick, respectively. The pinholes in
the sheets were about 0.1 mm in diameter and arranged in about
1.0 × 1.0 mm arrays. Curve B exhibits much lower ﬂow rate
dependence than curve A at the expense of sensitivity. Combination
of Equations 1 and 2 results in
In practice, Ik is not only potential dependent but also effected by
subtle surface changes often observed in prolonged service time.
The term which is most easily to control in Equation 3 is Id that can
be controlled in manufacturing by using resistive elements with
well-deﬁned parameters, such as perforated Teﬂon plates. Equation
the sensor output current I will become practically Id which is ﬂow
rate independent and linear with the ozone concentration in the
3.3. Linearity and Response Time
Figure 4 presents the concentration dependence of the steady
state sensor output and Figure 5 gives the transient responses. It is
seen that with increasing resistance of the diffusion barrier, the
linear range was expanded but the sensitivity was reduced and the
response slowed. In Figure 4, line A has a slope of 1.6 mA/ppm cm2
with a linear range up to about 160 ppm compared with the slope of
0.3 mA/ppm cm2 over the whole range studied (0–350 ppm) for line
B. The 95 % response times for the cases A and B in Figure 5 are 3 s
The residual current density was ca. 0.1 mA/cm2 with a noise
level of ca. 50 nA/cm2. For a signal to noise ratio S=N ¼ 3, the lower
detection limit for A is estimated to be ca. 0.2 ppm.
Based on the metal-Naﬁon electrode techniques, a solid-state
amperometric sensor was made for measuring ozone concentration
in gaseous samples. This sensor showed good linearity and fast
response. Special attention was paid to the ﬂow rate effect on the
sensor response. The ﬂow rate effect can be depressed by using a
diffusion barrier. An increase in the resistance of the diffusion
barrier will reduce the ﬂow rate inﬂuence and broaden the linear
range but, at the same time, decrease the sensitivity and slow down
transient response. This sensor is suitable for portable ozone meters.
In view of the ﬂow rate effect, a sampling pump may improve the

[1] R.G. Rice, A. Netzer, Handbook of Ozone Technology and Application, Ann
Arbor Science, Ann Arbor, MI, Vol.1, 1982.
[2] G. Schiavon, G. Zotti, G. Bontempelli, G. Farma, G. Sandona, Anal. Chem.
